messages.content:
You are an expert EU Horizon Europe proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions — a photonics and NIR spectroscopy SME in Barcelona.

Write in this exact style:
- First person plural throughout: we, our, the project will, we will develop
- Vary sentence length: short declarative statement, then longer supporting sentence
- Domain-specific vocabulary with grounded numbers and reasoning chains
- For work plan sections: bold task labels — **Task 2.1** — with partner attribution: (Lead: IRIS; Partners: X, Y)
- For objectives: numbered, specific, measurable, linked to TRL advancement
- NEVER use: poised to deliver, transformative outcomes, holistic approach, dynamic interplay, at the forefront, leverages its expertise

Write the State of the Art section (1.2) for a Horizon Europe proposal on: advanced vitrimers, fibre-optic sensing, circular composites and sustainable materials. Cover the current landscape, recent advances with citations, remaining gaps, and why this research direction is necessary.

state of the art, each with distinct limitations. Fibre Bragg 
grating (FBG) sensors offer excellent integration and multiplexing but face a diameter-robustness trade-off and 
prohibitive interrogator costs (€10–50k per unit)19,20. Distributed fibre-optic sensing (DFOS) extends measurement 
quasi-continuously, but shares cost barriers21. Piezoelectric sensors excel at impact detection but not quasi-static 
strain measurement22. Self-sensing composites using conductive networks (CNT, graphene, MXenes) offer 
multifunctionality but suffer signal drift and insufficient quantitative resolution23. Wire-based resistive sensing 
provides low-cost readout but remains limited to point measurements24,25. 
A critical missing dimension is integration of sensing with actuation for autonomous response. Vitrimer self-healing 
has achieved ≥ 90% modulus recovery in laboratory settings but requires manual external heating with no pathway 
for autonomous in-situ activation. TFP proves a platform for functional integration of sensors, wires, and heating 
elements during preform manufacture26,27,28 but integration of electronic/micro-electronic elements and scalable 
digital workflows remain open challenges. Gap: No fully embedded, cost-effective sensing-actuation system exists 
for composite construction that combines distributed sensing with localised thermal actuation, on-board processing, 
and energy-autonomous operation at industrial manufacturing speeds. Beyond the SoA. MOSAIC will develop fully 
embedded sensing-actuation systems manufactured within composite components during pultrusion and LCM. The 
innovation integrates resistive wire-based sensors with analogue front-end conditioning, MCU-based edge analytics, 
energy-aware power management, and low-power wireless communication (BLE, LoRa, CAN). Metallic sensor and 
heating networks will be incorporated via TFP with positional accuracy below 2 mm. Local thermal actuators co-
located with the sensor network will trigger vitrimer self-healing autonomously, targeting actuation to 120 °C in 
under 60 s at power consumption below 10 mW per node. A TFP-enabled design-to-manufacturing workflow will 
convert 3D load-bearing geometries into process-specific representations, integrating structural design, sensor 
placement, and assembly into a single continuous design logic. (Linked to O2, O3, O5) 
iv)Digital intelligence. SoA and gaps. Most digital twin implementations for civil infrastructure operate at 
descriptive to predictive maturity levels, visualising sensor data and generating predictive alerts, with few validated 
instances incorporating uncertainty quantification, prescriptive feedback loops, or coupling to autonomous 
 
14 Laachachi, A., Coiffier, F., Leroy, M.-A., Ruch, D., & Laheurte, P. (2025). Use of expandable graphite as debonding on demand technology between glass fibre reinforced 
polyamide 6 and aluminium. Journal of Advanced Joining Processes. DOI: 10.1016/j.jajp.2025.100369 
15 Jordan, A., Hermelingmeier, L., Gilich, J., Meschut, G., De Santis, M., & Schlüter, A. (2025). Comparison of the economic efficiency and sustainability of two debonding 
processes for structurally bonded sills. Journal of Advanced Joining Processes, 12, 100332. DOI: 10.1016/j.jajp.2025.100332 
16 Cheng, X. et al. (2021). Enabling contactless rapid on-demand debonding and rebonding using hysteresis heating of ferrimagnetic nanoparticles. Materials & Design, 210, 110130. 
DOI: 10.1016/j.matdes.2021.110130 
17 Bibi, I., Sikandar Ali Baig, M., Quayyum Bari, M. A., Subhani, T., & Aamir, M. (2023). A comprehensive study of electrically switchable adhesives: Bonding and debonding on 
demand. Materials Today Communications, 106293. DOI: 10.1016/j.mtcomm.2023.106293 
18 Stamp, C.-H., Stumpp, J., & Calvino, C. (2025). Adhesives with debonding-on-demand capability: Leveraging responsive microcapsules for mechanically-induced debonding. 
Advanced Materials, 37(13), 2414308. DOI: 10.1002/adma.202414308 
19 Zhan

You are an expert EU Horizon Europe proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions — a photonics and NIR spectroscopy SME in Barcelona.

Write in this exact style:
- First person plural throughout: we, our, the project will, we will develop
- Vary sentence length: short declarative statement, then longer supporting sentence
- Domain-specific vocabulary with grounded numbers and reasoning chains
- For work plan sections: bold task labels — **Task 2.1** — with partner attribution: (Lead: IRIS; Partners: X, Y)
- For objectives: numbered, specific, measurable, linked to TRL advancement
- NEVER use: poised to deliver, transformative outcomes, holistic approach, dynamic interplay, at the forefront, leverages its expertise

Write the State of the Art section (1.2) for a Horizon Europe proposal on: circular polyurethane recycling, chemical depolymerisation, and bio-based PU synthesis. Cover the current landscape, recent advances with citations, remaining gaps, and why this research direction is necessary.

state of the art in circular polymer 
chemistry, NIPU systems, digitalised materials development and closed-loop recycling technologies, including 
through joint activities with other EU-funded projects. 
Communication toolbox and channels: To achieve these objectives, a dedicated Communication Toolbox will be 
developed and maintained, consisting of the following: 
■ Visual identity and branding: A project logo has been developed in proposal preparation. This will form the basis 
of a brand book with visual guidelines to ensure consistency across all outputs; for building a professional image. 
■ Project website and social media: The project website will serve as the central communication hub, hosting public 
deliverables, news, blog content (e.g. >4 Thought Leadership articles and >2 white papers), ≥ 6 newsletters and 
multimedia content. Content will be amplified via consortium partners’ established social media channels (e.g. 
LinkedIn, X), complemented by dedicated NIPULOOP channels (LinkedIn and YouTube) and targeted hashtags 
(#NIPULOOP, among others) to maximise reach.  
■ Informative and multimedia materials: Printable and digital materials (flyers, posters, banners, application notes, 
white papers) and multimedia assets (introductory and explainer videos) tailored to different audiences. 
■ Industry-facing communication: Participation in ≥6 relevant trade fairs and exhibitions, targeted outreach, face-
to-face meetings and direct engagement with industrial stakeholders and infrastructure operators (and materials such 
as Application Notes, Datasheets, will be prepared). 
■ Professional and trade publications: ≥6 Articles in professional magazines, online platforms and technical blogs 
addressing industrial and practitioner audiences. 
■ Public relations: Press releases aligned with key milestones and results, disseminated through appropriate 
channels, including EU-level platforms and initiatives such as Materials Commons for Europe, IAM4EU and Made 
in Europe, as well as relevant sectoral networks including CLEPA, CEPE, Plastics Europe, Cefic and the Circular 
Plastics Alliance, ensuring alignment with industrial, regulatory and data ecosystem developments.” 

HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-05:  
Circular innovative advanced materials: facilitating the transition from design to markets  
 
Page 22 of 48

our communication strategy, their expected interests in NIPULOOP and an outline of the key communication 
messages that will be used in communication campaigns to each.

Write the State of the Art section (1.2) for a Horizon Europe proposal on: CO2 capture, methanol-to-olefins conversion, and industrial carbon utilisation. Cover the current landscape, recent advances with citations, remaining gaps, and why this research direction is necessary.

state of the art (SoA) MtO, KPI 1.2: Heat 
Integration Effectiveness >75%, KPI 1.3: Water Recovery Ratio >85%, KPI 1.4: Validation of 2 industrial use 
cases (ethylene/propylene + Li2CO3)/ End-user validation. 
Related WPs: All 
O2 
To prove the financial viability of CCU-based feedstocks by reaching a competitive levelized cost of 
production. 
Outcomes: European strategic autonomy; lower OPEX for chemical/ceramic sectors. 
KPI 2.1: 30% reduction in operation expenses for Ethylene/Propylene vs. fossil benchmarks, KPI 2.2: reduction 
of >25% of costs associated to CO2 from commercial sources for production of lithium carbonate. 
Related WPs: WP6, WP8 
O3 
To advance the patented CSF reactor and the integrated V-CaL/AEM chain from TRL 4-5 to a 
validated industrial environment. 
Outcomes: De-risked investment for large-scale CCU deployment. 
KPI 3.1: 500+ hours of cumulative operating campaigns at TRL 6, KPI 3.2: Operational logs and Certification of 
Product Quality by ORL/TOR. 
Related WPs: WP4, WP5, WP6 
O4 
To enhance the robustness of the Vacuum Calcium Looping capture process and maximize sorbent 
lifetime while maintaining high CO2 capture efficiency and purity under continuous industrial 
operation 
Outcomes: Reduced energy penalties for CO2 purification. 
KPI 4.1:  Up to 500% increase in effective Ca-based sorbent lifetime under sulphur-containing flue gas conditions, 
KPI 4.2: CO2 capture efficiency >90%, KPI 4.3: CO2 purity in captured stream >99%- tested under 2 
representative flue gas compositions. 
Related WPs: WP1, WP2, WP3, WP4 
O5 
To advance the patented CSF reactor from lab-scale to a continuous pilot unit (TRL 6) for olefin 
synthesis. 
Outcomes: Breakthrough in carbon-to-chemical conversion rates. 

 
 
        HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 
Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products 
Page 4 of 48 
KPI 5.1: 100% increase in CO2 conversion per pass; KPI 5.2: >50% increase in yield toward Ethylene (C2H4) and 
Propylene (C3H6), KPI 5.3: Sorbent Segregation Efficiency >90%, KPI 5.4: System Throughput 30 Nm3/h, KPI 
5.5: Operational stability >100h, KPI 5.6: Carbon Utilisation Efficiency >90% 
Related WPs: WP1, WP2, WP3, WP4, WP5 
O6 
To upscale HydroLiC CCU as a sustainable process for conversion of CO2 into high added value 
Li2CO3 with industrial purity at kg/h rates and validate it for use as an active material and as an 
additive for ceramics. 
Outcomes: Circular raw material sourcing; reduced virgin lithium dependency. 
KPI 6.1: Li2CO3 purity >98 wt.%, KPI 6.2: Extraction of >60% of lithium from black mass with no usage of acids 
or redox agent, KPI 6.3: utilisation of 450 kg of CO2 for the processing of >2.000 kg of active material, KPI 6.4: 
Validation in ceramic frits and active materials 
Related WPs: WP1, WP2, WP3, WP4, WP5 
O7 
To develop and validate a high-fidelity Digital Twin (DT), based on a multi-agent system 
architecture, to enable system-level optimisation, energy-efficiency assessment and industrial 
deployment of the integrated Capture-to-Product CCU platform, including dynamic synchronisation 
with variable renewable energy supply. 
Outcomes: Synchronisation of the capture, electrolysis, and conversion units; Accelerated process optimisation 
and predictive maintenance; Stable operation under fluctuating renewable energy conditions. 
KPI7.1:  Prediction deviation ≤10% for key process variables (CO2 conversion, energy consumption, product 
yield) compared to pilot data, KPI7.2: Integration of ≥5 core subsystems into the DT (capture, purification, CSF 
reactor, sorbent loop, H2 supply / electrolysis, energy system) and evaluation of ≥5 industrial deployment scenarios 
through open-loop simulation, KPI7.3: Demonstration of ≥10% improvement in selected system performance 
indicators (energy efficiency, yield, or cost) and delivery of a validated DT-based decision-support framework for 
replication and scale-up 
Related WPs: WP6 
O8
